blocks.text: I looked up and saw a man with a measur- ing line in his hand.

1 : A , see 1.16n. Such a line was used to determine divisions within a particular territory as in Ezek 40.3, a text that also foresees the rebuilding of Jerusalem and the Temple, using the categories of and .
Then I asked, “Where are you going?” He answered me, “To measure Jerusalem, to see what is its width and what is its length.”
2 : Zechariah's questions presume that, in this vision, he understands what he sees.
Then the angel who talked with me came forward, and another angel came forward to meet him,
and said to him, “Run, say to that young man: Jerusalem shall be inhabited like villages without walls, because of the multitude of people and animals in it.

4 : Restoration focuses on , not the Temple, as was the case in Ezekiel. Here the absence of walls stands in stark contrast with the walls described in Ezek 40. Jerusalem's walls were eventually rebuilt (Neh 2.17–6.15).
For I will be a wall of fire all around it, says the Lord, and I will be the glory within it.”
2.1–5: The third vision: a surveyor measures Jerusalem.
5 : , the Persian capital Pasargadae stood without walls and was surrounded by fire altars. God as will be located within the fiery circumference; cf. Ezek 43.2,4.
Up, up! Flee from the land of the north, says the Lord; for I have spread you abroad like the four winds of heaven, says the Lord.
Up! Escape to Zion, you that live with daughter Babylon.

6–7 : , Heb “hoy” is elsewhere often translated as “Woe,” though here it simply calls attention to what is being said; cf. Isa 1.24; Jer 27.6. , the place where Israelites are in exile and from which they will return, so Jer 3.18; 16.15; 23.8; 31.8. , cf. Jer 50.42; 51.33; Ps 137.8.
For thus said the Lord of hosts (after his glory sent me) regarding the nations that plundered you: Truly, one who touches you touches the apple of my eye.
8a : Lit., “after glory sent me to the nations.” Cf. vv. 9,11, which also report that the
8b : , the pupil.
See now, I am going to raise my hand against them, and they shall become plunder for their own slaves. Then you will know that the Lord of hosts has sent me.
9 : , another bodily metaphor of the deity. Only when the nations are plundered will Israelites know that the sent Zechariah.
Sing and rejoice, O daughter Zion! For lo, I will come and dwell in your midst, says the Lord.
10 : , cf. Isa 12.6; Zeph 3.14–15. In all three texts, the command to sing accompanies the presence of the God .
Many nations shall join themselves to the Lord on that day, and shall be my people; and I will dwell in your midst. And you shall know that the Lord of hosts has sent me to you.
The Lord will inherit Judah as his portion in the holy land, and will again choose Jerusalem.
12 : does not occur elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible, but see Wis 12.3; 2 Macc 1.7. Zechariah emphasizes the sacred character of the land of Israel.
Be silent, all people, before the Lord; for he has roused himself from his holy dwelling.
1.1–8.23: Oracles and visions attributed to Zechariah. This first major division of the book has three parts, each introduced by a date formula (1.1; 1.7; 7.1).
2.6–13: Admonitions and promises.
13 : , cf. Hab 2.20; Zeph 1.7. Sacrifices at the Temple were conducted in silence. is consistent with v. 11. can refer to either the earthly temple (Ps 26.8) or the heavenly abode (Deut 26.15).
